request.question: When did Limp Bizkit become a rap rock band?

title: Three Dollar Bill, Y'all
Limp Bizkit
Icon (Limp Bizkit album)
Eat You Alive
Ready to Go (Limp Bizkit song)
Results May Vary
Limp Bizkit discography
Significant Other
Stampede of the Disco Elephants
Home Sweet Home/Bittersweet Symphony
sentences: Three Dollar Bill, Y'all$ is the debut studio album by American rap rock band Limp Bizkit.

Released on July 1, 1997, the album established the band's trademark sound with the singles "Counterfeit", which was influenced by hip hop and heavy metal, and "Faith", a cover of a song by George Michael.
Limp Bizkit's rearrangement of the song incorporated heavier guitar playing by Wes Borland and scratching by DJ Lethal.
Limp Bizkit is an American rap rock band from Jacksonville, Florida, formed in 1994.
Their lineup consists of Fred Durst (lead vocals), Sam Rivers (bass, backing vocals), John Otto (drums, percussion), and Wes Borland (guitars, backing vocals).
Their music is marked by Durst's angry vocal delivery and Borland's sonic experimentation.
Borland's elaborate visual appearance, which includes face and body paint, masks and uniforms, also plays a large role in the band's elaborate live shows.
The band has been nominated for three Grammy Awards, have sold 40 million records worldwide and won several other awards.
Icon is the fourth compilation album by American rock band Limp Bizkit.

Released in 2011, it is a retrospective compiling material from the band's albums "Three Dollar Bill, Yall$", "Significant Other", "Chocolate Starfish and the Hot Dog Flavored Water", "Results May Vary" and "The Unquestionable Truth (Part 1)".
"Eat You Alive" is a song by the band Limp Bizkit.
It was released in September 2003 as a single from their fourth studio album "Results May Vary" (2003).
The song was written by Fred Durst, John Otto, Sam Rivers and Mike Smith, and is Limp Bizkit's first single without Wes Borland, who had left the band in 2001.
"Ready to Go" is a song by the American nu metal band Limp Bizkit.
The single features rapper and then label-mate Lil Wayne and is produced by Polow Da Don.
The single is Limp Bizkit's first release for Cash Money Records after their departure from Interscope in 2011.
The song was released in March 2013 as a free download on the band's website and, on April 16, as a digital single on iTunes and Amazon.
Results May Vary is the fourth studio album by the American rap rock band Limp Bizkit.
Released on September 23, 2003, it is the band's only release under the sole-leadership of vocalist Fred Durst after the temporary departure of guitarist Wes Borland, who left the band in 2001.

Snot guitarist Mike Smith was initially brought in to replace Borland, although the band's falling-out with Smith later lead to his departure, with much of the material recorded with him being discarded from the final release.
Durst and a number of guests ended up handling the majority of the album's guitar work.

The discography of Limp Bizkit, an American nu metal band, consists of five studio albums, three compilation albums, one remix album, one live album, one extended play, 26 singles, three promotional singles, 27 music videos and two video albums.
Limp Bizkit formed in 1995 in Jacksonville, Florida.
The band has sold an estimated 50 million albums worldwide.
Significant Other is the second album by American rap rock band Limp Bizkit.
Released in 1999 by Flip/Interscope Records, the album saw the band expanding its sound from that of its debut album "Three Dollar Bill, Yall", to incorporate further metal and hip hop influences.
"Significant Other" was co-produced by Terry Date and Limp Bizkit.
The album has sold at least 16 million copies worldwide.
Stampede of the Disco Elephants is the upcoming sixth studio album by American nu metal band Limp Bizkit.
It is set to be Limp Bizkit's first album to be released since DJ Lethal's departure in May 2012.
After the original line-up reunited in 2009, "Gold Cobra" was released in 2011.
The band left Interscope and signed up with Cash Money Records in February 2012.
The first single "Lightz" was released on October 26, 2012.
After many delays, the second single for the album, "Ready to Go", featuring label mate Lil Wayne was released on April 16, 2013, to positive reviews.
It is the first album since "The Unquestionable Truth (Part 1)" to be produced by Ross Robinson.
"Home Sweet Home / Bittersweet Symphony", also known as "Bittersweet Home" is a song by American rock group Limp Bizkit.
It was released in December 2005 as the only single from the compilation album "Greatest Hitz".
The song is a medley of "Home Sweet Home" by Mötley Crüe and "Bitter Sweet Symphony" by The Verve.

It is, along with the songs "Why" and "Lean On Me", the only new content on the "Greatest Hitz" compilation, and is the third cover song Limp Bizkit has released on an album (the others being The Who's "Behind Blue Eyes", and George Michael's "Faith").

candidate.summarize1.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `passages`, produce the fields `summary`.

Task: Compress the grounded first-hop evidence into a single concise, retrieval-oriented intermediate state to guide the next-hop retrieval. Do not produce the final answer or perform any new retrieval here.

Required content of `summary` (single field):
- A short header identifying the primary topic(s) found in the passages (1 line).
- A concise enumeration of the concrete facts supported verbatim by the provided passages: list entity mentions and directly supported factual spans (dates, years, titles, roles). Use very short quoted fragments (<= ~15 words) only when those words appear verbatim in the passages. Keep to facts present in the passages; do not add or infer new facts.
- Explicitly expose any ambiguity or missing information that blocks answering: if multiple candidate entities or page variants appear, enumerate those candidates and label the name as ambiguous; if a required target fact is missing or ambiguous, label it as the missing fact and identify the bridge entity that connects this hop to the next.
- Prioritized next-hop retrieval directives (1–4 items): concrete page titles or disambiguation pages to fetch, explicit article sections to inspect (e.g., lede/infobox, "Career", "Publication", "History"), or tight search keywords. Order them by priority and be specific (include possible disambiguation terms or section names).

Formatting and style constraints:
- Keep the entire `summary` concise: a short header plus 1–4 brief sentences or 2–6 short bullet lines in total.
- Preserve the habit of extracting and citing concrete facts verbatim from the passages (names, dates, passage labels).
- Do not output a final answer, comparison, or any numeric fact that purports to resolve the question here. Even if the passages fully and unambiguously contain all required facts, present that evidence and explicitly state "no additional retrieval needed," but defer producing any final answer to a downstream component.
- If a name or term has multiple distinct variants in the passages, do not pick one without disambiguating evidence; enumerate candidates instead.
- Do not invent or add facts not present in the supplied passages. Avoid long verbatim quotations; prefer short extracts (<= ~15 words) or paraphrase.

Output expectations: produce exactly one field `summary` containing the concise, retrieval-oriented summary that meets the above requirements.
candidate.create_query_hop2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, produce the fields `query`.

Produce exactly one compact, retrieval-focused second-hop query in the field `query`. Use only the provided `question` and `summary_1` as inputs. Do not include any explanation, answer, fallback text, lists, or additional fields — the output must be a single one-line query string.

Decision templates (choose the single template that matches the missing evidence implied by summary_1):
- Connector between two named items: emit a short interrogative naming both items and requesting the linking evidence (e.g., artist album recording) in one line. Retain the visible item tokens from the inputs.
- Metadata for a single anchor title: emit a compact keyword-style query consisting of the anchor plus brief attribute keywords (for example: artist genre).
- Temporal or aggregate fact: include the year or season token plus the entity type and a short cue such as season count.

Strict formatting rules:
- Emit exactly one line and exactly one string in `query` containing only the query text.
- Keep the query compact (aim for ≤ 10–12 words/tokens) and free of punctuation or explanatory clauses.
- Preserve visible tokens from `question` and `summary_1` (title words, years, or entity names) to anchor retrieval.
- Do not answer, conclude, enumerate candidates, assert hidden facts, or include user-facing instructions.

Produce the `query` field now.
candidate.summarize2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `context`, `passages`, produce the fields `summary`.

Instructions (must follow exactly):
- Emit a single field named `summary` containing only 1–3 concise sentences that integrate the first-hop `context` and the second-hop `passages` into a grounded post-retrieval state.
- If the `context` already contains a concise answer sentence, keep that sentence as the first sentence of `summary` unless the `passages` clearly contradict it; append supporting sentence(s) after it rather than replacing it.
- Preserve the primary answer name(s) exactly as they appear in the inputs (context or passages) unless those names clearly conflict with stronger passage content.
- When one or more `passages` supply short biographical, membership, partnership, or corroborating facts about a named entity from the `context`, append at most one brief supporting sentence per relevant named person. Each such supporting sentence should:
  - begin with the entity's canonical name as shown in the passages (if available),
  - paraphrase up to one or two key attributes present in the passages (examples: nationality, award/winner status, current affiliation/team, partner identity, founding/formation fact, birth date), and
  - avoid long verbatim copying (prefer paraphrase; use short quotes only when a brief exact phrase from a passage is necessary for clarity).
- If the question requires naming both members of a partnership or collaborator pair, name both people in the summary and, if passages provide partner-specific attributes, include one brief attribute for each as separate short clauses or sentences if needed.
- Do not invent facts not present in the provided passages. If passages conflict on a specific attribute, do not synthesize a new reconciled fact; instead prefer the passage that most directly addresses the question or omit the disputed attribute (or state necessary ambiguity only when needed to avoid a false claim).
- Keep the `summary` compact and focused: avoid extraneous biography, discography/chart data, or unrelated commentary. When `context` is absent or is not a concise answer, produce a 1–2 sentence integrated summary naming the relevant entity(ies) and including at most one brief paraphrased attribute drawn from the passages.

Output requirement: exactly one JSON field `summary` whose value is the integrated 1–3 sentence summary.
candidate.final_answer.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, `summary_2`, produce the fields `answer`.

Instructions:
- Use only `summary_1` and `summary_2` as the evidential basis for the final decision; do not add new facts or external information.
- First determine whether the question is a binary (yes/no) proposition and whether both `summary_1` and `summary_2` clearly and unambiguously support the same binary outcome.
- If BOTH summaries unambiguously assert the same binary conclusion, emit exactly one lowercase token as the entire content of `answer`: use "yes" for an affirmative conclusion and "no" for a negative conclusion. The `answer` field must contain that single token and nothing else (no punctuation, no extra whitespace, no explanation).
- Prefer `summary_2` as the decisive statement when it clarifies or restates `summary_1`, but still require that both summaries support the same binary judgment before emitting the single-token `answer`.
- If the summaries conflict, if either summary hedges, is ambiguous, silent about the binary relation, or otherwise fails to clearly agree, do NOT apply the single-token rule; instead produce the module's normal fallback/unresolved output for `answer`.
- Always remain truthful to the visible summaries and do not alter their factual conclusions.

Released on July 1, 1997, the album established the band's trademark sound with the singles "Counterfeit", which was influenced by hip hop and heavy metal, and "Faith", a cover of a song by George Michael.  Limp Bizkit's rearrangement of the song incorporated heavier guitar playing by Wes Borland and scratching by DJ Lethal.

Significant Other | Significant Other is the second album by American rap rock band Limp Bizkit.  Released in 1999 by Flip/Interscope Records, the album saw the band expanding its sound from that of its debut album "Three Dollar Bill, Yall", to incorporate further metal and hip hop influences.  "Significant Other" was co-produced by Terry Date and Limp Bizkit.  The album has sold at least 16 million copies worldwide.

Limp Bizkit | Limp Bizkit is an American rap rock band from Jacksonville, Florida, formed in 1994.  Their lineup consists of Fred Durst (lead vocals), Sam Rivers (bass, backing vocals), John Otto (drums, percussion), and Wes Borland (guitars, backing vocals).  Their music is marked by Durst's angry vocal delivery and Borland's sonic experimentation.  Borland's elaborate visual appearance, which includes face and body paint, masks and uniforms, also plays a large role in the band's elaborate live shows.  The band has been nominated for three Grammy Awards, have sold 40 million records worldwide and won several other awards.

Released in 2005, it is a retrospective compiling material from the band's albums "Three Dollar Bill, Yall$", "Significant Other", "Chocolate Starfish and the Hot Dog Flavored Water" and "Results May Vary".

Icon (Limp Bizkit album) | Icon is the fourth compilation album by American rock band Limp Bizkit.  Released in 2011, it is a retrospective compiling material from the band's albums "Three Dollar Bill, Yall$", "Significant Other", "Chocolate Starfish and the Hot Dog Flavored Water", "Results May Vary" and "The Unquestionable Truth (Part 1)".

Collected (Limp Bizkit album) | Collected is the third compilation album by American rock band Limp Bizkit.  Released in 2008, it a retrospective compilation album from the band's albums "Three Dollar Bill, Yall$", "Significant Other", "Chocolate Starfish and the Hot Dog Flavored Water", "Results May Vary" and "The Unquestionable Truth (Part 1)".

Limp Bizkit discography | The discography of Limp Bizkit, an American nu metal band, consists of five studio albums, three compilation albums, one remix album, one live album, one extended play, 26 singles, three promotional singles, 27 music videos and two video albums.  Limp Bizkit formed in 1995 in Jacksonville, Florida.  The band has sold an estimated 50 million albums worldwide.
response.summary_1:
Header: Limp Bizkit — formation years, genre labels, and early album dates

- Factual spans from passages:
  - "Limp Bizkit is an American rap rock band from Jacksonville, Florida, formed in 1994." (passage [3])
  - Passage [7] states a conflicting formation: "formed in 1995 in Jacksonville, Florida." and calls them "an American nu metal band." (passage [7])
  - The debut album: "Three Dollar Bill, Y'all$ is the debut studio album by American rap rock band Limp Bizkit." and "Released on July 1, 1997." (passage [1])
  - The second album: "Significant Other is the second album by American rap rock band Limp Bizkit. Released in 1999." (passage [2])
  - Compilation pages variably label them "American rock band Limp Bizkit." (passages [4], [5], [6])

- Ambiguities / missing facts blocking the answer:
  - Conflicting formation year: candidate formation years are 1994 (passage [3]) and 1995 (passage [7]) — "formed in 1994" vs "formed in 1995 in Jacksonville, Florida."
  - Inconsistent genre labels: passages call the band "rap rock," "nu metal," and "rock" — unclear when (or whether) the band was first described specifically as "rap rock."
  - Missing target fact: an explicit date/year/period stating when Limp Bizkit "became" or was first labeled a "rap rock" band (bridge entity: Limp Bizkit band history/infobox/early-years).

- Prioritized next-hop retrieval directives:
  1. Fetch the Wikipedia page "Limp Bizkit" — inspect the lede/infobox and the "History" or "Early years" section for formation year, first genre descriptions, and timeline of stylistic development.
  2. Fetch "Three Dollar Bill, Y'all" (album) page — inspect lede/infobox and "Background" or "Release" sections to see how/when the band and album were described (genre labels, dates).
  3. Fetch "Significant Other" (album) page lede/infobox for genre labeling and release context (1999).
  4. Fetch "Limp Bizkit discography" lede for formation year and genre summary; if needed, search keywords: "Limp Bizkit formed 1994 1995 rap rock origin timeline."

response.summary_2: {"summary":"Limp Bizkit is an American rap rock band from Jacksonville, Florida, formed in 1994. Their debut album Three Dollar Bill, Y'all was released on July 1, 1997, helping establish the band's rap-rock sound, and their second album Significant Other followed in 1999."}
response.answer: According to the summaries, Limp Bizkit formed in 1994 (some sources in summary_1 give 1995), and their debut album Three Dollar Bill, Y'all (released July 1, 1997) helped establish the band's rap-rock sound.
